literal: doing, performing, the verbal action itself; from √kṛ
invariant_definition: *Kriyā* names the verbal action itself — the dynamic process expressed by a finite verb — as distinct from its agent (*kartṛ*), instrument (*karaṇa*), or patient (*karman*/*kārya*). In Pāṇinian grammar a sentence is built around a *kriyā* whose role-relations are filled by the *kāraka*s; *kriyā* is therefore the relational center that the *kāraka*-roles surround. In ritual and Vedāntic discourse *kriyā* often picks out enjoined ritual performance under *codanā* (Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā) — but the term is broader, covering any process intelligible through agent-instrument-patient analysis. The *kriyā / kāraka / phala* triad shows how the Indian schools refused to treat 'action' as a primitive: every action is already a relational structure with its grammar and its causal residue. In Tantric and Śaiva contexts *kriyā* further names the *kriyā-pāda* of the Āgamas — the ritual-procedural section, contrasted with *jñāna-pāda* (doctrinal), *yoga-pāda* (meditative), and *caryā-pāda* (conduct). Vedāntic schools differ on whether *kriyā* in its ritual sense is co-instrumental in liberation (the *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* dispute), but they all accept the Pāṇinian grammatical analysis as the descriptive baseline for what an action is.

per_school.definition: In Advaita, kriyā belongs to the body-linga-self imagined through vāsanā and therefore cannot be predicated of the ātman disclosed by `neti neti`. (cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.11.14) Vedic and Smārta kriyā retain a preparatory place only insofar as they subserve ātmalābha; they are not co-instrumental with liberating knowledge. (cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.4)

In Tattva-vāda, no kriyā proceeds independently of Hari, and the very occurrence of action is read under divine dependence. (cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.14) The same locus is deployed against the thesis that mokṣa could arise from jñāna-svabhāva alone, so kriyā and its fruit remain theistically governed rather than autonomous. (cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.14)

In Sāṃkhya, kriyā marks the side of the vyakta: the manifest is characterized as `sakriya`, alongside being caused, impermanent, composite, and dependent. (cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/10) The school also uses ordinary `kriyāsu` as a teleological analogue for prakṛti's own pravṛtti, which proceeds for the sake of puruṣa's release. (cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/58)

In Yoga, `kriyā` first appears soteriologically as `kriyā-yoga`, namely tapas, svādhyāya, and īśvarapraṇidhāna. (cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.1) It also appears cosmologically in the definition of the dṛśya as `prakāśa-kriyā-sthiti-śīla`, where activity names the rājasic moment within the guṇa-complex oriented to bhoga and apavarga. (cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.18)

In Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, kriyā is treated under the padārtha `karma`, enumerated as five motions: upward movement, downward movement, contraction, expansion, and locomotion. (cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.6) This karma is a distinct real inhering in a single substance and functioning as the cause of conjunction and disjunction. (cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.16)

In Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā, dharma is the artha marked by codanā, so action is grasped under the regime of Vedic injunction rather than as a merely physical happening. (cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.2) Śabara makes the point explicit by glossing codanā as speech that impels kriyā, with the agent seen saying `ācāryacoditaḥ karomi`. (cite://shabara/mimamsa-bhasya/1.1.2)

For Mādhyamaka, kriyā does not stand as an independently established entity either with conditions or without them. (cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.4) Accordingly, action belongs with agent, instrument, cause, effect, arising, cessation, and fruit among the dependently designated items whose svabhāva is denied. (cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.17)

In Yogācāra, the decisive question is `kṛtyakriyā`, practical efficacy: without it, bare appearance could not count as an object. (cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/2) Vasubandhu nevertheless argues that such efficacy can be established within consciousness-only, as dream-affection and hell-experience show, so kriyā need not secure external-object realism. (cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/and Hell)

In Pratyabhijñā / Trika, kriyā is a śakti of consciousness itself: when cit contracts into the bound state, kriyā-śakti contracts from universal agency into limited doership. (cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/9-vrtti) The same contraction yields the karmic field as `śubhāśubhānuṣṭānamayaṃ kārmaṃ malam`, so action is read as a diminished mode of Śiva's own svātantrya rather than as something external to consciousness. (cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/9-vrtti)

translator_note: 'Action' for *kriyā* is acceptable but loses the verb-centred relational structure that Pāṇinian and Mīmāṃsā analysis presuppose. 'Process' is sometimes better. The standard mistranslation collapses *kriyā* and *karma* into a single English 'action,' whereas in technical Sanskrit they are distinct: *kriyā* is the verbal process; *karman* is the patient role within it (or, derivatively, the residue the action produces). Translators who flatten the distinction destroy the entire *kāraka*-grammar argument.
